title: Direct provision & other intervention
summary:
## The big idea Taxes nudge prices; price controls fix them. But sometimes the government skips the market entirely and **does the job itself** — building the school, running the hospital, employing the soldiers. And sometimes it doesn't touch production at all, but changes the *rules* (regulation), the *information* people have, or even the *way choices are presented* (nudges). This chapter completes your policy toolkit — and introduces the idea that lets you evaluate every tool in it: **government failure**. ## Direct (state) provision With **direct provision** the government produces and supplies goods itself, usually **free at the point of use** and funded from taxation. It is the natural answer for two kinds of good: - **Public goods** (defence, street lighting, flood defences): non-excludable and non-rival, so private firms can't charge for them — the free-rider problem means markets barely provide them at all. - **Merit goods** (education, healthcare): markets *under*-provide them because people underestimate the benefits and the positive externalities spill over to everyone else. State schools and public hospitals push consumption up and guarantee access **regardless of income**. **The catch — three standard problems.** First, the **opportunity cost**: every clinic is a road not built; provision must be paid for by taxes that have their own distorting effects. Second, the **zero-price problem**: at a price of zero, quantity demanded races ahead of what budgets can supply, so rationing reappears as **waiting lists** (a familiar story in the UK's NHS) — rationing by *time* instead of price. Third, **inefficiency**: without competition or a profit motive, state producers may let costs drift up (**x-inefficiency**) and respond slowly to what users actually want. ## Transfer payments **Transfer payments** — pensions, unemployment benefit, child support — redistribute income **without any good or service being produced in return**. They attack poverty and inequity directly and let recipients choose what to buy (unlike provision in kind). The worries: cost to the budget, and the risk that poorly designed benefits blunt the **incentive to work** (why take a job if benefits are withdrawn as fast as wages arrive?). ## Regulation and legislation Rules that **compel or ban**: minimum school-leaving ages, emissions limits, bans on selling cigarettes to minors, seat-belt laws, licensing of doctors. - **Strengths:** direct and certain compared with a tax (a ban on leaded petrol removes it entirely; a tax merely discourages it); works where price signals are weak. - **Weaknesses:** enforcement and monitoring are costly; blunt (the same rule hits everyone, whatever their costs of complying); can create black markets; and regulators can be **captured** by the industry they police — writing rules that suit producers, not the public. ## Information provision Where the failure is **information** (consumers don't know cigarettes harm them, or which loan is cheapest), the cheapest fix is to provide the missing information: warning labels, nutrition labelling, anti-smoking campaigns, compulsory disclosure of interest rates. It preserves consumer choice and costs little — but people may **ignore** it, effects arrive slowly, and habit or addiction can defeat mere facts. ## Behavioural 'nudges' A **nudge** changes the *presentation* of choices — the **choice architecture** — without banning anything or changing prices: enrolling workers into pension schemes **by default** (they can opt out, most don't), putting fruit at eye level, printing tax letters that say 'most people in your town pay on time'. Nudges are cheap and freedom-preserving, and default effects in particular are powerful. But effects are often small, they can wear off, and critics ask whether it is legitimate for the state to steer choices covertly. (A2 behavioural economics develops this.) ## Government failure — the master evaluation idea Intervention is *supposed* to fix market failure, but it can leave society **worse off**: that is **government failure**. Its classic causes: - **Imperfect information** — the government can't measure the externality or know 'the right' level of provision any better than the market can. - **Unintended consequences** — rent caps that shrink housing supply; benefit rules that discourage work; taxes that spawn smuggling. - **Administrative and enforcement costs** that eat the welfare gain. - **Regulatory capture** and lobbying. - **Political short-termism** — policies chosen for the next election, not the next generation. The exam skill: never conclude 'the government should intervene' or 'shouldn't' in the abstract. Compare the **size of the market failure** with the **likely size of the government failure**, for the specific tool proposed.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Direct provision**: state produces, free at use — for **public goods** (free-rider problem) and **merit goods** (equity + externalities). - Its problems: **opportunity cost**, **zero-price → excess demand → waiting lists**, **x-inefficiency**. - **Transfer payments**: redistribute income, nothing produced in return; watch work incentives. - **Regulation**: compel/ban — certain but blunt; enforcement costs; capture risk. - **Information provision**: fixes information failure cheaply; may be ignored. - **Nudges**: change choice architecture (defaults!); cheap, freedom-preserving, effects can be small. - **Government failure**: intervention that makes things worse — imperfect info, unintended consequences, costs, capture, short-termism. - Match the **tool to the failure**, then weigh market failure vs government failure.

keyTerms.definition: Government producing and supplying a good itself, often free at the point of use.
A government payment that redistributes income with no good or service produced in return.
Rules that compel or prohibit certain behaviour to correct market failure.
Government action to correct information failure (labels, warnings, campaigns).
Designing how choices are presented to influence behaviour while preserving freedom of choice.
When intervention creates a net welfare loss, e.g. through distortions, costs or unintended effects.

diagrams: No specific diagram, but direct provision of a merit good links to the **positive-externality (MSB > MPB)** diagram — reference it to show the welfare gain from higher consumption.
tips: Match the **tool to the failure**: public goods → direct provision; information failure → information/nudges; inequity → transfer payments.
Always evaluate with **government failure** — costs, unintended consequences, and opportunity cost of spending.
For direct provision, note the **zero-price problem**: excess demand, waiting lists and rationing by time not price.
Use nudges/choice architecture as a modern, high-level evaluation point (links to A2 behavioural economics).
Balance every answer: state the intended benefit, then the risk of government failure, then a judgement.

channels.why: Covers state provision, regulation and government failure.

channels.name: EconplusDal
channels.why: Good synthesis of intervention tools and evaluation.

revisionNotes:
# Direct provision & other intervention — quick revision

**Match the tool to the failure**
- Public goods (free-rider) → **direct provision**.
- Merit goods / positive externalities → provision, subsidy.
- Information failure → **information provision**, labelling, campaigns; or **nudges**.
- Inequity/poverty → **transfer payments**.
- Demerit goods / negative externalities → tax, **regulation**, bans.

**Direct provision**
- Free at point of use, tax-funded; guarantees access.
- Problems: opportunity cost; zero price → **excess demand & waiting lists** (rationing by time); **x-inefficiency** without profit motive.

**Transfer payments** — income redistributed, nothing produced; may weaken work incentives.

**Regulation** — certain and direct, but blunt; costly to enforce; black markets; **regulatory capture**.

**Information provision** — cheap, preserves choice; can be ignored; slow.

**Nudges** — choice architecture (default pension enrolment); cheap and liberty-preserving; small/fading effects; ethical debate.

**Government failure (evaluation core)**
- Causes: imperfect information, unintended consequences, admin/enforcement costs, capture, political short-termism.
- Exam judgement: weigh the market failure against the likely government failure *for the specific tool*.
questions:
# Practice questions — Direct provision & other intervention **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** Which of the following is a transfer payment? - A — a teacher's salary paid by the state - B — a state pension paid to a retired worker - C — government spending on a new motorway - D — a subsidy paid to a solar-panel producer
▸ Show answer

B. A transfer payment redistributes income with nothing produced in return. A teacher's salary buys a service (teaching); the motorway buys construction; the subsidy buys output.

**2.** A government provides healthcare free at the point of use. The most likely consequence is: - A — excess supply of healthcare - B — excess demand and waiting lists - C — a market-clearing price - D — under-consumption of healthcare
▸ Show answer

B. At a price of zero, quantity demanded exceeds what the budget can supply, so care is rationed by waiting time instead of price.

**3.** Government failure occurs when: - A — the government fails to intervene in a market - B — a market fails to reach equilibrium - C — intervention results in a net loss of economic welfare - D — a government loses an election
▸ Show answer

C. Government failure = intervention that leaves society worse off overall, e.g. through unintended consequences or costs that exceed the corrected market failure.

**4.** Consumers persistently underestimate the benefits of adult education courses. The intervention that most directly targets this failure is: - A — a maximum price on courses - B — an information campaign about the returns to education - C — a tax on employers - D — a ban on unskilled work
▸ Show answer

B. The root failure is information. Providing the missing information targets the cause; the other tools distort prices or quantities without correcting beliefs.

## Paper 2 style — data response > A middle-income country introduces free school meals for all primary pupils, funded from general taxation. Take-up is far higher than forecast and some schools run out of food. An opposition politician argues the \$300 million cost should instead be paid as cash transfers to the poorest families, who 'know their own needs best'. A newspaper notes that school attendance has risen 6% since the scheme began. **(a)** Define 'direct provision'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain why take-up of the free meals exceeded the forecast. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse **two** reasons why a government might provide school meals rather than leave nutrition to the market. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether the government should replace the scheme with cash transfers to poor families. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) The government producing/supplying a good itself, typically free at the point of use, funded by taxation. [1+1]

(b) Zero price → quantity demanded exceeds planned supply; families who previously paid for lunches switch in; demand at P=0 is far beyond the forecast based on partial take-up. [4]

(c) (i) Merit good with positive externalities — better-fed children learn more, raising future productivity (note the attendance rise); (ii) equity — guarantees poor children eat regardless of household income; add information failure (parents may under-value nutrition). Any two, developed. [3+3]

(d) For transfers: choice, targeting the poorest is cheaper, less waste. Against: cash may not be spent on children's food (information failure/behaviour); universal meals remove stigma and reach every child; attendance externality is tied to meals at school specifically. Judgement: hybrid (targeted transfers + school provision) or defend one side with the evidence given. [8]

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain why governments directly provide public goods and merit goods rather than relying on markets. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether government intervention in markets always improves economic welfare. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Public goods: non-excludability → free-rider → missing market. Merit goods: under-consumed (information failure + positive externalities); provision raises consumption and equity.

(b) For: corrects genuine failures (public goods, externalities, information). Against: government failure — imperfect information, unintended consequences, costs, capture, short-termism; examples (rent controls, fuel subsidies). Judgement: intervention improves welfare only when the failure is large and the tool well-designed — case-by-case, not always.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain how regulation and information provision can each be used to reduce consumption of a demerit good such as tobacco. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether 'nudges' are a better way to change consumer behaviour than regulation. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Regulation: age limits, advertising bans, smoking bans in public places — compulsion. Information: health warnings, plain packaging, campaigns — correcting misperceived private costs.

(b) Nudges: cheap, preserve freedom, powerful defaults (pension enrolment), politically easy. But: small/fading effects, covert-manipulation concerns, weak against addiction. Regulation: certain and enforceable but blunt, costly, liberty-reducing, black-market risk. Judgement: depends on the behaviour — nudges for inertia problems, regulation where harms are severe; often best combined.
